museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: A museum case for the month when PlayStation queues, N64 rumble, PC strategy, import chatter, and modem patience all shared the same carpeted room.
museum.curatorNote: This researched draft treats October 1997 as a UK-aware exhibit: the month of Lylat Wars and Rumble Pak on British shelves, Edge issue 50 at the newsagent, and a wider release calendar whose exact dates often shift by region.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: Five moments that made October feel less like a date on a calendar and more like a threshold between boxed media and networked play.

items.title: Castlevania: Symphony of the Night finally reaches North America
items.body: Konami's PlayStation gothic adventure arrived in North America at the start of the month, while European players were still waiting for the PAL release. In hindsight it looks like a museum object from the future: 2D craft surviving inside the 3D boom.
items.certainty: verified date range; sources differ by one day
items.sourceNote: Castlevania release-date listings give North America as October 2 or October 3, 1997; Europe follows in November 1997.

items.title: Fallout opens the vault
items.body: Interplay's post-nuclear PC RPG launched in North America for MS-DOS and Windows. It was not a loud console-window event in Britain, but for PC players it made moral choice, text, and consequence feel newly radioactive.
items.sourceNote: Fallout Wiki and Wikipedia both list October 10, 1997 for the original Fallout release.

items.title: Age of Empires makes history playable
items.body: Microsoft and Ensemble Studios released Age of Empires, turning ancient history into a CD-ROM ritual of villagers, tech trees, and skirmishes that could swallow an evening at the family PC.
items.sourceNote: Age of Empires references list October 15, 1997 as the first release date.

items.title: Lylat Wars and the Rumble Pak land in Europe
items.body: Nintendo UK's Lylat Wars page lists a 20 October 1997 release. For British N64 owners, the game made vibration feel less like a novelty and more like a new sense built into the controller.
items.sourceNote: Nintendo UK lists Lylat Wars for Nintendo 64 with a 20/10/1997 release date.

items.title: Edge issue 50 turns the newsagent into a preview room
items.body: Future Publishing's Edge issue 50, dated October 1997, gathered previews and CD-ROM material for Grand Theft Auto, Zelda 64, Tomb Raider 2, Panzer Dragoon Saga, Quake, and more. In the UK, that kind of cover-mounted media was part news, part shrine.
items.sourceNote: The Centre for Computing History listing for Edge issue 50 records its October 1997 publication date and preview contents.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight notable October-window releases. Dates are regional where possible; uncertain or conflicting entries are marked rather than smoothed over.

items.title: Castlevania: Symphony of the Night
items.body: North American PlayStation release; European release followed in November. A 2D landmark arriving while the industry was talking almost entirely in polygons.
items.certainty: verified date range; not UK release
items.sourceNote: Castlevania Wiki lists October 2, 1997 for North America; Wikipedia lists October 3, 1997.

items.title: Fallout
items.body: North American PC release of Interplay's post-apocalyptic role-playing game, notable for tone, consequence, and a harsher kind of humour than most fantasy RPGs.
items.sourceNote: Fallout Wiki and Wikipedia list October 10, 1997.

items.title: Age of Empires
items.body: The first Age of Empires made ancient history into a real-time strategy toybox, complete with scenario-making and multiplayer potential.
items.sourceNote: Age of Empires references list October 15, 1997.

items.title: G-Police
items.body: Psygnosis' sci-fi police craft game belongs firmly to the autumn 1997 UK PlayStation mood: ambitious, cinematic, and slightly fogged by draw distance.
items.sourceNote: Push Square lists a UK/Europe PlayStation date of 15 October 1997; VGMdb lists 24 October 1997 for Europe. Kept as uncertain.

items.title: Lylat Wars
items.body: The European Star Fox 64 release, bundled in memory with the Rumble Pak sensation even when the accessory itself became the bigger playground story.
items.sourceNote: Nintendo UK lists Lylat Wars release date as 20/10/1997.

items.title: Monopoly Star Wars
items.body: A very 1997 artefact: the Star Wars Special Edition moment translated into a CD-ROM board game with multimedia sheen.
items.sourceNote: Monopoly Star Wars references list October 21, 1997.

items.title: Fighting Force
items.body: Core Design and Eidos pushed the belt-scroller into polygonal streets. The exact feel was modern arcade bravado: big fists, blocky bodies, and a British studio name on the box.
items.sourceNote: Giant Bomb, IMDb, and Grouvee list October 31, 1997; treated as sourced secondary data.

items.title: Crash Bandicoot 2: Cortex Strikes Back
items.body: Naughty Dog's sequel sat on the edge of October in many modern listings, though reliable sources conflict between October 31 and early November for North America. UK players were still looking ahead to the December PAL release.
items.sourceNote: Crash Bandicoot Wiki and IMDb list October 31, 1997; Wikipedia lists November 6, 1997 and Europe as December 5. Marked uncertain.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: The machines were teaching players new bodily habits: rumble, analogue sticks, graphics cards, and the etiquette of the household phone line.

items.title: Nintendo 64 Rumble Pak
items.body: By October, the European Rumble Pak moment arrived with Lylat Wars. It made impact tactile, but also made AAA batteries part of the ritual.
specs: Force feedback as novelty and promise
items.sourceNote: Rumble Pak references list Europe in October 1997; Nintendo UK lists Lylat Wars on 20/10/1997.

items.title: PlayStation Dual Analog
items.body: Sony's Dual Analog Controller had launched in Japan in April 1997, placing two sticks into the PlayStation conversation before the DualShock became the familiar standard.
items.sourceNote: Guinness World Records and PlayStation controller histories list the Dual Analog's Japanese release in April 1997.

items.title: 3dfx Voodoo and the PC upgrade itch
items.body: By late 1997, 3dfx Voodoo Graphics and Glide had become a shorthand for the PC owner who could make games look impossibly smooth, provided the family machine had the right card inside.
items.sourceNote: 3dfx histories and Glide manuals document the 1996-1997 Voodoo/Glide ecosystem; 3dfx was widely adopted by end of 1997.

items.title: Dial-up Britain
items.body: The internet was present but not ambient. UK reporting in October 1997 described roughly one in 25 households as having an internet connection, which meant online gaming still felt scheduled, precious, and interruptible.
specs: FAQs and server lists over always-on feeds
items.sourceNote: The Independent reported in October 1997 that one in 25 UK households had an internet connection; Ipsos later summarised 1997 home ISP access at about 5%.

sections.title: Magazine Covers
sections.subtitle: The newsagent shelf was the algorithm. Covers, preview discs, and tips booklets told you where the future was supposed to be.

items.title: Edge #50
items.headline: GTA, Zelda 64, Tomb Raider 2 and the year's biggest games
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed cover image for Edge issue 50, October 1997.
items.curatorNote: A UK Future Publishing issue that reads like a map of the next six months: Grand Theft Auto, Zelda 64, Tomb Raider 2, Panzer Dragoon Saga, Quake, and a CD-ROM preview culture all in one object.
items.body: A UK Future Publishing issue that reads like a map of the next six months: Grand Theft Auto, Zelda 64, Tomb Raider 2, Panzer Dragoon Saga, Quake, and a CD-ROM preview culture all in one object.
items.sourceNote: Centre for Computing History lists Edge issue 50, October 1997, with preview and CD-ROM contents.

items.title: N64 Magazine Issue 7
items.headline: Yoshi's Story, Diddy Kong Racing and GoldenEye import fever
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed cover image standing in for N64 Magazine issue 7, October 1997.
items.curatorNote: A very British N64 snapshot: Yoshi on the cover, Diddy Kong Racing nearly ready, UK reviews for Doom 64 and others, and GoldenEye import coverage glowing at 94%.
items.body: A very British N64 snapshot: Yoshi on the cover, Diddy Kong Racing nearly ready, UK reviews for Doom 64 and others, and GoldenEye import coverage glowing at 94%.
items.sourceNote: Nintendo Mags lists N64 Magazine issue 7 as October 1997 and summarises cover and contents.

items.title: Official Nintendo Magazine
items.headline: Diddy Kong Racing, GoldenEye 007, Zelda and a tips booklet
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed cover image for Official Nintendo Magazine, October 1997.
items.curatorNote: The cover as equipment: an official Nintendo licence, Diddy Kong Racing anticipation, GoldenEye presence, and a tips booklet physically attached to the issue.
items.body: The cover as equipment: an official Nintendo licence, Diddy Kong Racing anticipation, GoldenEye presence, and a tips booklet physically attached to the issue.
items.sourceNote: Centre for Computing History lists Official Nintendo Magazine October 1997 and notes the attached tips booklet.

items.title: Nintendo Power #101
items.headline: Extreme-G: Speed Zone Ahead
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed cover image for Nintendo Power issue 101, October 1997.
items.curatorNote: A North American counterpoint on the shelf: futuristic racing, Mace: The Dark Age, Mischief Makers, and the bright certainty that the N64 still had speed to sell.
items.body: A North American counterpoint on the shelf: futuristic racing, Mace: The Dark Age, Mischief Makers, and the bright certainty that the N64 still had speed to sell.
items.sourceNote: Original Magazines and Emuparadise listings identify Nintendo Power #101 as October 1997 with Extreme-G cover focus.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: Going online still felt like an event: switch on, dial in, wait, search, print, disconnect.

items.title: Dial-up was household negotiation
items.body: In Britain, the internet was still rare enough to feel special. The phone line mattered; a parent picking up downstairs could end the session as surely as a boss fight.
items.sourceNote: The Independent reported one in 25 UK households online in October 1997; Ipsos later summarised 1997 home ISP access at about 5%.

items.title: Server lists felt like treasure maps
items.body: QuakeWorld had already changed PC multiplayer, and tools around Quake server browsing helped define the shape of late-1990s online play. You found an address, copied it carefully, and hoped the ping behaved.
items.sourceNote: QuakeWorld references place its release in December 1996; GameSpy histories trace the QSpy/GameSpy server-browser lineage through 1996-1997.

items.title: FAQs were pocket guides
items.body: Game help moved through plain-text walkthroughs, Usenet posts, and fan pages. Many players still printed pages, folded them, and carried them back to the console.
items.certainty: contextual; not tied to a single October event
items.sourceNote: Included as cultural context rather than a dated news claim.

items.title: Cover discs were broadband by post
items.body: For many UK players, demo discs and magazine CD-ROMs were the most reliable way to encounter videos, playable demos, and previews without waiting on a modem.
items.sourceNote: Edge issue 50 is listed with a CD-ROM previewing major games; Official U.S. PlayStation Magazine also launched with demo-disc culture in late September/October 1997.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: A narrative rather than a timeline: October 1997 as remembered through shops, bedrooms, magazines, and the hum of hardware.

items.title: The future arrived unevenly
items.body: One house had a PlayStation, another had an N64, another had a PC that could just about run the new strategy game if nothing else was open. Visiting a friend could feel like crossing a platform border.

items.title: The UK shelf had its own weather
items.body: American dates appeared in magazines, Japanese imports haunted preview pages, and PAL releases landed when they landed. The waiting was not a bug in the culture; it was part of the culture.

items.title: Paper made games bigger
items.body: A cover line could keep a school lunch table busy for a week. An attached tips booklet could feel like an upgrade. A CD-ROM on the cover was not a supplement; it was a small door.

items.title: Everything was becoming touchable
items.body: Rumble, twin sticks, 3D cards, and online server lists all suggested the same thing: games were starting to push out of the screen and into the hands, the room, and the phone line.
